items.q: Where is our Solar System located in the Milky Way?
items.correct: About two-thirds of the way out from the centre, in one of the spiral arms.
items.misconception: Right at the centre of the galaxy.
items.why: Students assume our Sun is central (Copernican habits die hard). We're in an unremarkable position about 26,000 light years from the centre, in the Orion Spur, a minor branch of the Milky Way.

items.q: What defines a star as being on the 'main sequence'?
items.correct: It fuses hydrogen into helium in its core.
items.misconception: It is the largest stage of a star's life.
items.why: Students think main sequence = most spectacular. It's defined by the FUEL: hydrogen fusion. Stars spend most of their lives there because hydrogen is plentiful and fusion is stable.

items.q: What is a black hole's event horizon?
items.correct: The boundary beyond which not even light can escape.
items.misconception: The visible 'edge' of the black hole.
items.random_wrong: A literal horizon you can walk to.
items.why: Students picture a surface. The event horizon is a mathematical BOUNDARY - nothing is physically there. Once inside, escape requires faster-than-light speed, which is impossible.

items.q: Which galaxy type is most likely to have very active star formation?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Irregular. “Elliptical” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is at the centre of most large galaxies?
items.why: Correct answer: Supermassive black hole. “A neutron star” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In approximately how many billions of years will the Milky Way and Andromeda merge?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
